figures.caption: Figure 1: Sparse-data Maxwell Reconstruction with Exact Hidden-unit Structure. From sparse pointwise observations of a single field instance, FLASH-MAX fits a shallow trainable architecture whose hidden neurons are exact Maxwell solutions, so the predicted field solves Maxwell’s system throughout optimization. The resulting model is exact by construction, expressive on arbitrary domains, and yields accurate reconstruction from sparse data. Exactness is ensured by using computer algebra to obtain the hidden layer restriction z20 = z21 + z22 + z23 and the polynomials p(z) which tie the vectorial weights. Top-left: Sparse data sampled only at t = 0 (“×” symbols), showing that our model is predictive; data can also be sampled on the boundary of the validation domain (“•” symbols). Bottom: Quiver plot visualization of our fast prediction of an electromagnetic knot (Hopfion) at time t = 0.5. The difference to ground truth is indistinguishable.

Table 1: Experiment 1: Race to 5%. Validation Error, Residual Error, and Compute training time to reach 5% relative validation error (if possible) for the ground truths: Plane Waves, Radial Waves, Hopf Fibration, Random Solution with/without boundary conditions (BCs). Animations to complement these results visually are available in the supplementary material at flash_max/flash_max_gifs.

Figure 2: Experiment 2: Performance on a Time Budget (BC setup). The curves depict the achievable error rates (y-axis) given a specific time budget (x-axis) for the setting with boundary conditions. Uncertainties are shown (standard error of the mean over 5 random seeds). FLASH-MAX (ours, blue solid line) clearly converges significantly faster than S-EPGP across all four problems. FEM and PINN are excluded since they do not reach the PDE residual error threshold of 0.01 during the time budget (cf. Table 1), which is a bare minimum to claim fidelity to the physics. Plots for the IC setup can be found in the Appendix A, Figure 3 and follow a similar pattern.

Table 2: Experiment 3: Performance on a Data Budget. Sensitivity of FLASH-MAX to the number of training points (#pts) in the IC (initial conditions) setup. All runs use random seed 42. We write PW = Plane Waves, RW = Radial Waves, HF = Hopf Fibration, RS = Random Solution.

Table 3: FLASH-MAX’s sensitivity to network width (Width) and activation function (Activation). In all these runs, we use the Random Solution and a seed of 42. All other hyperparameters are fixed as in Table 1. Executed on a single NVIDIA L40S.

Table 4: The same FLASH-MAX experiments as were carried out in Table 1, but executed on a CPU, an INTEL(R) XEON(R) GOLD 6542Y. We allowed at most 10 minutes of training (600 seconds) to each experiment.

Figure 3: Experiment 2: Performance on a Time Budget (IC setup). The curves depict the achievable error rates (y-axis) given a specific time budget (x-axis) for the setting with initial conditions. Uncertainties are shown (standard error of the mean over 5 random seeds). FLASH-MAX (blue solid line) converges more reliably and usually significantly faster than existing methods across all four problems: Plane waves, Radial waves, Hopf fibration, and Random solution. Plots are analogous to those for the BC setup in Figure 2. While S-EPGP seems to perform comparably here, we note that it required an NVIDIA A100 to achieve this performance, while FLASH-MAX is achieving this performance on an NVIDIA L40S.
Figure 4: Graphical representation of the forward pass (3) of FLASH-MAX, here expressed much like in (FLASH-MAX) in terms of each timespace frequency zik = Zik• ∈ R4 for i = 1, 2, k = 1, . . . , 2W .
Table 6: Validation Error, Residual Error, and Compute training time to reach the best relative validation error.
Figure 5: Time evolution of the magnitude of the electric field for the Plane Waves solution.
Figure 6: Time evolution of the magnitude of the electric field for the Radial Waves solution.
Figure 7: Planar streamlines of the Hopf Fibration solution.
Figure 8: Time evolution of the magnitude of the electric field for the Random Solution.
